what_it_does: Implementation guide for the Universal Projection instrument: step-by-step HOW to make each Completion Criteria item true, grouped G1 through G11 plus small registries/gate/20/80, with cross-cutting principles, paid-for DON'Ts, and preservation constraints.
observations: Originated by Tim Geldard; all derived work attributed to him.
Built on RESEARCH-SYNTHESIS.md and uses an exact tense convention: 'IS' = exists, 'SHOULD BE' = planned.
The instrument is described as the geometric face of the company's resolution system, with four live mechanisms being the same resolve() call hardcoded.
No hardcoded sectors ever; divisions must resolve from data/registries, and the data-driven default is the non-negotiable floor.
Pure read floor: the instrument never emits resolve/approve/dispatch; strain/forbidden zones are rendered and surfaced to the operator.
Form is half of done: operator-facing surfaces must meet a design rubric via design-lint and a separate design-critic.
DON'Ts are lessons already paid for, each with a because.
Per-group HOW labels each change as REUSE or NET-NEW and names file:symbol seams.
G1 warns that tests/projections_acceptance.py tests the unrelated PLURAL lens registry, so a new suite tests runtime/projection.py:project.
G5 is a rebuild of LatticeView chrome on components/kit.tsx with real corpus tokens and migration of ~37 CSS literals.
G9 is a two-gravity separator gated on Model Call 2 and requires cross-lane coordination with the fabric owner.
A final section enumerates what each change must preserve before shipping.
standouts: The instrument is the same resolve(centre, spaces, n, k, audience, exhale) call with bindings freed; the long arc collapses four live mechanisms into one.
No hardcoded sectors ever; the data-driven default is the non-negotiable floor.
The instrument is a pure read: it never emits resolve/approve/dispatch and only surfaces strain/forbidden zones to the operator.
G1 acceptance suite must target runtime/projection.py:project, not tests/projections_acceptance.py, because the name collision hides missing instrument coverage.
G9 introduces a two-gravity separator using Tim-intent vs generic-AI instruction embeddings and a steering vector from Tim anchors.
conventions: Exact tense convention: 'IS' = exists, 'SHOULD BE' = planned.
Groups labeled G1–G11 with explicit REUSE vs NET-NEW classification.
Design compliance gated by design-lint plus a separate design-critic.
Colour = angle is the one deliberate literal-free geometric exception; everything else in the chrome is tokenised.
concerns: Many referenced capabilities are currently stubs or hardcoded: radius line 155 is a no-op, rings:4 is hardcoded, angle_from=<registry> is reserved-not-built, order_by=edges is unwired.
Heavy cross-file and cross-session dependencies: G8 requires operational coordination with retrieval/ops; G9 requires cross-lane fabric owner coordination.
G5 involves migrating ~37 CSS literals and verifying against design/_system/check.py plus a design-critic, which is a large surface for a single group.
Several groups are gated on external decisions or prior groups without clear fallback if gates are not met.
notes: A prescriptive, dependency-heavy implementation roadmap for the projection instrument, written as grouped work orders with strict reuse/net-new boundaries and a pure-read, data-driven floor.
questions: What is the exact current implementation state of runtime/projection.py:project and BindingRegistry?
What are the specific criteria and owner of Model Call 2 that gates G9?
What are the concrete Tim-intent anchor sets and generic-AI anchor sets for the two-gravity separator?
How does the event-to-registry-row edge use lineage/source_address in practice?
What is the precise algorithm for the 20/80 pressure-threshold type-birth/death water-law?
What is the full set of real corpus tokens in design/design-system.css and the expected output of design/_system/check.py?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is to tell HOW to make each Completion Criteria item true; the file does exactly that, organized as grouped implementation steps with principles, DON'Ts, and preservation constraints.
apparent_themes: Data-driven resolution with no hardcoded taxonomy
Geometric projection as the visible face of an underlying resolve call
Reuse over duplication; explicit net-new boundaries
Design/form parity as engineering debt, not polish
Incremental operational rollout with cross-lane coordination
Strain and forbidden zones as first-class rendered signals
intention_signals: Consolidate four hardcoded mechanisms into a single resolve-based projection instrument; replace stubs and hardcoded values with data-driven, tested, design-compliant components; keep the instrument a read-only renderer while surfacing strain and forbidden zones to the operator.
novelty: Treats the projection instrument as the geometric face of an underlying resolution call, with 'no hardcoded sectors' and 'pure read floor' as non-negotiable architectural constraints, and proposes a two-gravity separator between Tim-intent and generic-AI embeddings.
contribution: Provides a structured, principle-bound implementation plan that names exact seams (reuse vs net-new), defines acceptance boundaries, records paid-for lessons in DON'Ts, and enumerates preservation constraints so a fused system can avoid relearning them.
summary_for_embedding: Implementation guide for the Universal Projection instrument, grouping work G1–G11 with cross-cutting principles, DON'Ts, file:symbol seams, and preservation constraints.
intention_for_embedding: To direct incremental construction of a data-driven, read-only geometric projection UI while consolidating hardcoded mechanisms and preserving a pure-read floor.
